name: Mount Shasta
longDescription:
Siskiyou County, California, USA Mount Shasta is a towering stratovolcano rising 4,322 meters (14,179 feet) in northern California,
                dominating the landscape as a solitary, glacier-clad peak visible for over 100 miles. For the
                indigenous peoples of the region - including the Shasta, Wintu, Achumawi, Atsugewi, and Modoc -
                the mountain has been sacred since time immemorial, considered the home of the Great Spirit and
                the place where the Creator made the first people. In modern times, Mount Shasta has become a
                global center for New Age spirituality, attracting seekers who believe it is a powerful energy
                vortex, a UFO landing site, and the home of Lemurian survivors living in an underground city
                called Telos. Few mountains in the world attract such diverse and intense spiritual attention.

description:
Explore temples, mountains, groves, and holy sites that have served as gateways between the human and divine realms
                across cultures and millennia. From ancient stone circles to living pilgrimage destinations, discover the places
                where heaven touches earth.

geography: Elevated terrain with commanding views; often snow-capped or shrouded in mist, symbolizing proximity to the divine

keyMyths.title: The Creation Myth of the Klamath Tribes
keyMyths.description: According to Klamath tradition, Mount Shasta was created when the Chief of the Sky Gods descended to Earth and pushed snow and ice together to form the mountain. He then used a stick to create the trees and rivers, and the mountain became the home of the spirit world.
keyMyths.source: Klamath oral traditions, as documented in Barker, M. A. R. (1963). Klamath Dictionary. University of California Publications in Linguistics, 31.

keyMyths.title: The Lost Continent of Lemuria
keyMyths.description: In esoteric traditions, Mount Shasta is believed to be the location of Telos, a subterranean city inhabited by advanced beings from the lost continent of Lemuria. These beings are said to be highly evolved and maintain a connection to the spiritual realm.
keyMyths.source: Emerson, Willis George. The Smoky God, or, A Voyage to the Inner World. Forbes & Company, 1908.

keyMyths.title: Agartha and the Hollow Earth
keyMyths.description: Related to the Lemurian belief, some theories propose that Mount Shasta is one of the entrances to Agartha, a vast network of subterranean cities within the Hollow Earth. These cities are inhabited by ancient civilizations and possess advanced technologies.
keyMyths.source: Bernard, Raymond W. The Hollow Earth. Fieldcrest Publishing Company, 1969.

keyMyths.title: The Ascended Masters
keyMyths.description: New Age spiritual beliefs associate Mount Shasta with the Ascended Masters, spiritual teachers who have transcended earthly limitations. These masters are said to reside on the mountain in higher dimensional realms, offering guidance and wisdom to those who seek it.
keyMyths.source: King, Godfre Ray. Unveiled Mysteries. Saint Germain Press, 1934.

keyMyths.title: Home of the Little People
keyMyths.description: Local folklore, particularly among indigenous populations, speaks of the mountain as inhabited by the 'Little People' or elementals. These beings are often described as guardians of the mountain's sacred energy and are said to appear to those with pure intentions.
keyMyths.source: Loether, Christopher. Concepts of the Self in the Northern California Indian Languages. University of California, Berkeley. Survey of California and Other Indian Languages Report 7, 1987.

sources.name: Barker, M. A. R. (1963). Klamath Dictionary. University of California Publications in Linguistics, 31.
sources.description: A comprehensive dictionary of the Klamath language, containing valuable insights into Klamath culture and mythology, including creation stories related to Mount Shasta.
sources.title: Barker, M. A. R. (1963). Klamath Dictionary. University of California Publications in Linguistics, 31.

sources.name: Emerson, Willis George. The Smoky God, or, A Voyage to the Inner World. Forbes & Company, 1908.
sources.description: A fictional account that popularized the idea of a hollow Earth and subterranean civilizations, contributing to the myth of Lemuria's connection to Mount Shasta.
sources.title: Emerson, Willis George. The Smoky God, or, A Voyage to the Inner World. Forbes & Company, 1908.

sources.name: Bernard, Raymond W. The Hollow Earth. Fieldcrest Publishing Company, 1969.
sources.description: A compilation of theories and beliefs surrounding the Hollow Earth concept, often referencing Mount Shasta as a potential entrance.
sources.title: Bernard, Raymond W. The Hollow Earth. Fieldcrest Publishing Company, 1969.

sources.name: King, Godfre Ray. Unveiled Mysteries. Saint Germain Press, 1934.
sources.description: A key text in the I AM Activity, a New Age religious movement that promotes the belief in Ascended Masters and their presence on Mount Shasta.
sources.title: King, Godfre Ray. Unveiled Mysteries. Saint Germain Press, 1934.

sources.name: Loether, Christopher. Concepts of the Self in the Northern California Indian Languages. University of California, Berkeley. Survey of California and Other Indian Languages Report 7, 1987.
sources.description: An anthropological study of Northern California Indigenous cultures, including stories about elementals and spirits.
sources.title: Loether, Christopher. Concepts of the Self in the Northern California Indian Languages. University of California, Berkeley. Survey of California and Other Indian Languages Report 7, 1987.
